items.title: M0-workspace: Rust workspace and em-core crate
items.intent: A developer can run cargo test -p em-core and watch a real file round-trip through open, edit, save, and read back — no mocks, no stubs, no in-memory fake filesystem. This is the foundation everything else builds on.
items.description: Scaffold the Rust workspace and the em-core crate that all other components depend on. em-core owns the document model at this stage: a plain String-backed struct (NOT a piece table — that comes in Phase 0 post-M0). Expose exactly four public functions: open_file(path) -> Document, edit(doc, offset, delete, insert) -> (), save_file(doc, path), current_text(doc) -> &str. Write an integration test that (1) writes a temp file with known content, (2) opens it, (3) applies an edit, (4) saves, (5) reopens and asserts the edited content is present. No parser, no formatter, no diagnostics — explicitly out of scope for this item per PRODUCT.md §7.1.3.
steps: Run cargo init --name em-core in the em-core/ directory to create the library crate
Create a Cargo.toml workspace root with members = ["em-core"]
Define a Document struct in em-core/src/lib.rs holding a single String field
Implement open_file(path: &str) -> Result<Document, io::Error> using std::fs::read_to_string
Implement edit(&mut self, offset: usize, delete: usize, insert: &str) on Document — naive String splice for now
Implement save_file(&self, path: &str) -> Result<(), io::Error> using std::fs::write
Implement current_text(&self) -> &str returning the inner String slice
Write tests/integration_test.rs: create tmpfile with known content, open, apply an edit, save, reopen, assert edited content is present
Confirm cargo test -p em-core passes with zero failures

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo build --workspace exits 0 from the repo root
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo build --workspace

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo test -p em-core runs and passes, including an integration test that opens a real temp file, edits it, saves it, and reads the new content back from disk
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test -p em-core 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. [1-9]'

acceptanceCriteria.text: em-core exposes the four M0 public functions (open_file, edit, save_file, current_text) — proven by the integration test above calling each one
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test -p em-core 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. [1-9]'

acceptanceCriteria.text: Cargo.lock is committed (binary workspace convention)
acceptanceCriteria.verify: test -f Cargo.lock && git ls-files --error-unmatch Cargo.lock

items.title: M0-tauri-shell: Tauri 2.0 macOS app scaffold
items.intent: cargo tauri build produces a real .app bundle on macOS that opens a window with a webview. The build must succeed in CI without manual intervention. em-core is wired as a direct Rust dependency of the Tauri backend — no separate process, no mocks.
items.description: Scaffold a Tauri 2.0 project targeting macOS. The Tauri backend (src-tauri/) must depend on em-core from the workspace. The webview loads an HTML page (can be a blank page with a placeholder — the editor comes in M0-editor). The app must produce a working window that opens on launch. Wire CI (GitHub Actions) to build the Tauri app on macOS and verify the build artifact exists. This item does NOT include the editor, IPC bridge, or file operations — those come in subsequent M0 items.
steps: Run npm create tauri-app@latest or cargo tauri init in the repo root to scaffold the Tauri 2.0 project
Update the workspace Cargo.toml to include src-tauri as a member
Add em-core as a path dependency in src-tauri/Cargo.toml: em-core = { path = '../em-core' }
Verify the Tauri app compiles: cargo build -p em-markdown (or whatever the tauri crate is named)
Create a minimal src/index.html (or equivalent) — a div with 'Easy Markdown' text is sufficient for M0
Add a GitHub Actions workflow .github/workflows/build.yml that runs on push/PR, checks out code, installs Rust + Node deps, runs cargo tauri build on macOS runner
Confirm the workflow passes and produces a .app artifact

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo build --workspace completes successfully with src-tauri included and exits 0
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo build --workspace

acceptanceCriteria.text: src-tauri/Cargo.toml declares em-core as a path dependency
acceptanceCriteria.verify: grep -qE 'em-core.*path\s*=' src-tauri/Cargo.toml

acceptanceCriteria.text: The GitHub Actions build workflow exists, is valid YAML, and has at least one job
acceptanceCriteria.verify: test -f .github/workflows/build.yml && yq '.jobs | length >= 1' .github/workflows/build.yml | grep -q true

acceptanceCriteria.text: Tauri app builds a real bundle on the local machine
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cd src-tauri && cargo build --release

items.title: M0-bridge: Tauri IPC commands wiring em-core to the webview
items.intent: JavaScript in the webview can call invoke('open_file', {path}) and receive the file contents, call invoke('edit', ...) to mutate the document, and call invoke('save_file', ...) to persist it. Every bridge call goes through real em-core code — no in-memory stubs.
items.description: Expose the four em-core functions as Tauri IPC commands so the webview can call them. Each command must use the #[tauri::command] macro and be registered in the Tauri builder. The commands manage document state in the Tauri app state (AppState struct holding a Mutex<Option<Document>>). Write an integration test or end-to-end test that calls each command and verifies the result from the webview side. This item does NOT include the editor UI — only the IPC bridge and its test.
steps: Define AppState struct in src-tauri/src/main.rs with a Mutex<Option<em_core::Document>> field
Register AppState with .manage(AppState::default()) in the Tauri builder
Implement #[tauri::command] fn open_file(state, path) that calls em_core::open_file and stores the result in AppState
Implement #[tauri::command] fn edit(state, offset, delete, insert) that calls Document::edit on the stored doc
Implement #[tauri::command] fn save_file(state, path) that calls Document::save_file
Implement #[tauri::command] fn current_text(state) -> String that returns the stored doc's current_text
Register all four commands in .invoke_handler(tauri::generate_handler![...])
Write a Tauri integration test (or webview-side JS test) that exercises each command against a temp file

acceptanceCriteria.text: A Rust test exists in src-tauri that calls each of the four commands (open_file, edit, save_file, current_text) with a real AppState against a real temp file, and asserts each command returns the expected result
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test -p easy-markdown --test bridge 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. [4-9]|test result: ok\. [1-9][0-9]' || cargo test --workspace bridge 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. [1-9]'

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo test --workspace exits 0 with no failures
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok' && ! cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -qE 'FAILED'

acceptanceCriteria.text: A round-trip test asserts save_file writes bytes to disk that match what current_text returns
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test --workspace save_file_roundtrip 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. [1-9]'

items.title: M0-editor: CodeMirror 6 plain-text editor in the webview
items.intent: The webview displays a CodeMirror 6 editor instance. The user can type characters and see them appear immediately in the editor. No IPC yet — this item proves CodeMirror 6 is mounted, receives keystrokes, and maintains a local buffer.
items.description: Mount CodeMirror 6 in the Tauri webview with a minimal plain-text configuration. No markdown mode, no syntax highlighting, no decorations — raw text input only. The editor must be focusable, must accept keyboard input, and must maintain its content in a local JS buffer. Wire up a basic TypeScript/JS module that imports CodeMirror 6 packages via npm and initializes an EditorView. IPC bridge integration comes in M0-open-save-loop — this item ends when the editor is visible and accepts input.
steps: Add CodeMirror 6 packages as npm dependencies: @codemirror/view, @codemirror/state
Create src/editor.ts (or .js) that imports EditorView and EditorState from @codemirror/view and @codemirror/state
Initialize an EditorView with a minimal setup (basicSetup from codemirror package, or manual extensions list)
Mount the EditorView into a DOM element on index.html load
Verify the editor is visible in the webview and accepts keyboard input
Export a getContent() function from editor.ts that returns the current editor string content
Wire the build system (Vite or equivalent) to bundle the TypeScript/JS for the Tauri webview

acceptanceCriteria.text: npm install exits 0
acceptanceCriteria.verify: npm install

acceptanceCriteria.text: npm run build exits 0 and produces a dist/ directory with an index.html entry point
acceptanceCriteria.verify: npm run build && test -f dist/index.html

acceptanceCriteria.text: The built JS bundle contains CodeMirror EditorView code, proving @codemirror packages were imported and not tree-shaken away
acceptanceCriteria.verify: grep -rql EditorView dist/ | head -1 | grep -q .

acceptanceCriteria.text: The built bundle does NOT contain @codemirror/lang-markdown — M0 is explicitly plain-text-only per PRODUCT.md §7.1.6
acceptanceCriteria.verify: ! grep -rqlE 'lang-markdown|markdownLanguage' dist/

items.title: M0-open-save-loop: End-to-end open, edit, save via IPC
items.intent: A user can launch the app, open a .md file via the native OS file dialog, type characters that appear instantly, press Cmd+S to save, and reopen the file to see their edits persisted. All nine steps from PRODUCT.md §7.1.1 must work with real files on a real filesystem. Silent failure at any step is unacceptable.
items.description: Wire the CodeMirror 6 editor to the Tauri IPC bridge so that (1) clicking Open invokes the native file dialog and loads the file content into the editor, (2) keystrokes in the editor update the editor buffer, (3) Cmd+S (macOS) / Ctrl+S (Linux/Windows) calls the save_file IPC command to write the current editor content to disk. This is the completion of the M0 walking skeleton. Implement an end-to-end test that exercises all nine steps from the press release journey using Tauri WebDriver or a headless script.
steps: Add @tauri-apps/api as an npm dependency for the invoke() and dialog.open() APIs
Add a toolbar or menu with an Open button that calls dialog.open({filters: [{name: 'Markdown', extensions: ['md']}]}) and then invoke('open_file', {path})
On successful open_file response, call editor.dispatch() to replace the editor content with the returned text
Wire a keydown listener for Cmd+S / Ctrl+S that calls editor.state.doc.toString() and invoke('save_file', {path, content})
Update the save_file IPC command in Rust to accept the content string from JS rather than reading from AppState (simpler for M0)
Wire the native menu File > Open and File > Save to the same JS handlers
Write an end-to-end test: create a temp .md file, launch app, open file, type text, save, read the file from disk, assert new text is present

acceptanceCriteria.text: A named end-to-end test exists that launches the bridge, opens a temp file, applies an edit, saves, reopens, and asserts the new content is on disk
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test --workspace e2e_open_edit_save_reopen 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. [1-9]'

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo test --workspace passes every test (no ignored, no filtered failures)
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok' && ! cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -qE 'FAILED|test result: FAILED'

acceptanceCriteria.text: The Tauri app builds successfully for macOS
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cd src-tauri && cargo build --release 2>&1 | tail -5 | grep -qE 'Finished|Compiling'

items.title: M0-baseline: Capture performance baseline and lock regression gate
items.intent: docs/baseline.json exists, is committed, and contains real measured values for all six metrics from PRODUCT.md §7.1.5 on a documented reference machine. The CI job fails if a subsequent commit regresses any metric by more than 10%. Until this item is done, no Phase 1 or §7.2 feature item may be marked readyForSprint.
items.description: After M0-open-save-loop passes, run a measurement pass on the canonical baseline environment (GitHub Actions ubuntu-latest runner, not a local machine) and capture six metrics: cold startup to editable cursor, open 100-line file, open 10k-line file, keystroke-to-onscreen, save round-trip, memory footprint after 1 min editing a 10k-line file. Each metric is the median of N>=5 measurement runs. Write the results plus runner metadata (OS image version, uname -a, /proc/cpuinfo CPU model, free -m memory) into docs/baseline.json. Add a GitHub Actions job that runs the same measurement pass on every PR targeting main and fails the job if any metric median exceeds 110% of the committed baseline median. Local runs on other machines are for exploration only and do not count toward baseline comparisons. See PRODUCT.md §7.1.5 for the canonical environment decision and the methodology.
steps: Write a shell or Rust script that launches the app, performs each of the six operations with timing, and outputs JSON
Run the script on the reference machine and capture the six values
Write docs/baseline.json with the six metrics plus machine metadata (hostname, OS version, CPU, RAM)
Add a CI job step that runs the measurement script and parses the output
In the CI job, compare each measured value against docs/baseline.json; fail the job if any value exceeds 110% of the baseline
Commit docs/baseline.json with a clear commit message indicating this is the M0 baseline
Update the CI workflow to include the regression gate job on every PR targeting main

acceptanceCriteria.text: docs/baseline.json exists and contains all six metric fields plus runner metadata
acceptanceCriteria.verify: test -f docs/baseline.json && jq -e '.metrics.cold_startup_ms and .metrics.open_small_ms and .metrics.open_10k_ms and .metrics.keystroke_ms and .metrics.save_ms and .metrics.memory_mb and .runner.os_image and .runner.uname' docs/baseline.json

acceptanceCriteria.text: docs/baseline.json metrics are captured as medians, not single values (each metric has a runs array and a median)
acceptanceCriteria.verify: jq -e '.metrics.cold_startup_ms | type == "number" and (.metrics.cold_startup_ms.runs // [1,2,3,4,5] | length) >= 5' docs/baseline.json 2>/dev/null || jq -e '.metrics | to_entries | all(.value | type == "number")' docs/baseline.json

acceptanceCriteria.text: The GitHub Actions workflow contains a baseline job that reads docs/baseline.json and exits non-zero on a median regression >10%
acceptanceCriteria.verify: yq '.jobs.baseline or .jobs.baseline-regression' .github/workflows/*.yml 2>/dev/null | grep -qv null && grep -rq 'baseline.json' .github/workflows/

acceptanceCriteria.text: The baseline job runs the real Tauri app end-to-end on the runner (not a mock) and produces timing output parseable by the gate
acceptanceCriteria.verify: grep -rq 'cargo tauri build\|tauri-driver\|cargo run' .github/workflows/

items.title: Phase0-tree-sitter: Integrate tree-sitter-markdown into em-core
items.intent: em-core can parse any markdown string into a typed AST using tree-sitter-markdown (split_parser branch). The parse result is a Rust struct tree, not a raw tree-sitter CST. Every downstream feature (formatting, doctor, WYSIWYM decorations) reads from this AST — no component parses markdown ad-hoc.
items.description: Integrate tree-sitter-markdown (the split_parser branch, which separates block and inline grammars) into em-core as the single parser. Port the AST node types to Rust enums that map to the tree-sitter node kinds. The AST types must cover: headings, paragraphs, code blocks (fenced and indented), lists and list items, blockquotes, tables (GFM), links, images, emphasis, strong, inline code, thematic breaks, and frontmatter (YAML block detection). Provide a parse(text: &str) -> SyntaxTree function in em-core. Do NOT use the String-based Document from M0 for the AST — the parser operates on a &str input and returns an owned tree. Reference for algorithm inspiration (not code): the legacy Swift files in reference/ (TreeSitterConversion.swift and the EMFormatter/EMDoctor rule files). Note that reference/ does not contain an EMParser directory — use TreeSitterConversion.swift as the tree-sitter node-type mapping reference.
steps: Add tree-sitter and tree-sitter-markdown as Cargo dependencies (using the split_parser branch via git dependency)
Create em-core/src/ast.rs defining Rust enums for all major node kinds listed in the description
Create em-core/src/parser.rs implementing parse(text: &str) -> SyntaxTree that walks the tree-sitter CST
Map tree-sitter node type strings (e.g. 'atx_heading', 'fenced_code_block') to the Rust AST enums
Handle GFM extensions: table, strikethrough, task list item, autolink
Handle YAML frontmatter: detect a leading '---' block and wrap it in a FrontmatterNode
Write unit tests in em-core/src/parser.rs that parse representative markdown fragments and assert on the resulting AST structure
Run cargo test -p em-core and confirm all new parser tests pass

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo test -p em-core --test parser passes with at least 8 distinct test cases (heading, paragraph, fenced code, bullet list, ordered list, link, strong, emphasis)
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test -p em-core --test parser 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. ([8-9]|[1-9][0-9])'

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo tree lists tree-sitter and tree-sitter-md (or equivalent) as direct dependencies of em-core
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo tree -p em-core 2>&1 | grep -qE 'tree-sitter( |-md)'

acceptanceCriteria.text: parse() returns a typed AST with at least the major node kinds — not a raw tree-sitter cursor
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test -p em-core --test parser returns_typed_ast 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. [1-9]'

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo test --workspace exits 0 with no failures after integrating the parser
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok' && ! cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -qE 'FAILED'

items.title: Phase0-engine-decision: Decide and implement the post-M0 document model
items.intent: The document model engine decision is made against real baseline numbers, not speculation. ARCHITECTURE.md §Document Model lists three candidates (A piece table, B rope, C keep String). The FEAT-006 baseline.json exists and is authoritative. This item runs the three candidates against the same measurement script, picks the winner on the defensible criterion (the one that does not regress any baseline metric by more than 10% AND has the lowest complexity cost), and lands the chosen implementation. The four em-core public functions (open_file, edit, save_file, current_text) remain unchanged from M0.
items.description: Do not assume the answer. Run the measurement script from FEAT-006 against three implementations of em-core::Document: (A) a piece table with a balanced BST of pieces over original + add buffers, (B) a rope using the ropey crate, (C) the M0 String as a control. Commit the three measurement runs to docs/engine-comparison.json. Pick the implementation that does not regress any baseline metric by more than 10% AND has the lowest complexity cost (fewest added lines, no new unsafe, no platform-specific code). Default to C (keep String) unless A or B demonstrably beats it on a metric that matters for the product scope. Write a one-paragraph decision record in docs/engine-decision.md explaining the chosen candidate and the measured numbers that justified it. Replace em-core/src/lib.rs::Document with the chosen implementation. All M0 integration tests must continue to pass unchanged.
steps: Read docs/baseline.json (committed by FEAT-006) and the measurement script it references
Create three temporary branches or modules: engine_a_piece_table, engine_b_rope, engine_c_string_control
Implement candidate A (piece table) in engine_a_piece_table — pieces in a BTreeMap<usize, Piece>, Original and Add buffers, incremental line-starts
Implement candidate B (rope) in engine_b_rope using the ropey crate
Leave candidate C as the existing String implementation
Run the FEAT-006 measurement script against each candidate on the canonical ubuntu-latest environment, with N>=5 runs each
Write docs/engine-comparison.json with the three candidates median numbers for each metric
Pick the winner per the decision criterion in the description
Write docs/engine-decision.md explaining the choice
Replace em-core/src/lib.rs::Document with the chosen candidate
Run cargo test --workspace and confirm all M0 tests still pass
Delete the two losing candidate modules

acceptanceCriteria.text: docs/engine-comparison.json exists and contains median measurements for all three candidates on all six baseline metrics
acceptanceCriteria.verify: test -f docs/engine-comparison.json && jq -e '.candidates | length == 3 and (all(.metrics | length >= 6))' docs/engine-comparison.json

acceptanceCriteria.text: docs/engine-decision.md exists and names the chosen candidate with a justification referencing measured numbers
acceptanceCriteria.verify: test -f docs/engine-decision.md && grep -qiE 'chosen|winner|decision' docs/engine-decision.md

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo test --workspace passes — every M0 integration test still green after the engine swap
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. [1-9]' && ! cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -qE 'FAILED'

acceptanceCriteria.text: The measurement run on the chosen candidate does not regress any baseline.json metric by more than 10%
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo run -p em-core --example baseline_check 2>&1 | grep -qiE 'no regression|within budget'

items.title: Phase0-formatting-engine: Rule-based formatting engine in Rust
items.intent: em-core can format any markdown document in under 50ms for a 10k-line file, applying the five core rules. Every rule is implemented from scratch in Rust using the EMFormatter Swift algorithms as reference. No Swift code transfers. The output is a Vec<Mutation> describing byte-range replacements — the caller (CodeMirror bridge) applies them, the engine does not mutate documents directly.
items.description: Implement the formatting engine in em-core/src/formatter.rs. The engine runs five ordered rules ported from the algorithm logic in reference/EMFormatter/: (1) list continuation — auto-indent on Enter at list item, (2) table alignment — normalize column widths and separator row dashes, (3) heading spacing — ensure blank line before each heading unless at document start, (4) blank line separation — ensure blank line between paragraphs and block elements, (5) trailing whitespace trim — remove trailing spaces from all lines. The engine takes a parsed SyntaxTree (from FEAT-007) and the document text, and returns Vec<Mutation> where Mutation = {offset: usize, delete: usize, insert: String}. Rules are applied in order; later rules see the pre-mutation offsets (mutations are not applied in-place during formatting). A public format(tree: &SyntaxTree, text: &str) -> Vec<Mutation> function is the entry point. Reference the Swift algorithms only — do not call swift build.
steps: Create em-core/src/formatter.rs with a Mutation struct {offset, delete, insert} and a format() entry point
Implement Rule 1: list continuation — detect list items in the AST, compute correct indentation for next line
Implement Rule 2: table alignment — scan table nodes, compute max column widths, pad cells with spaces
Implement Rule 3: heading spacing — scan heading nodes, check preceding byte for blank line, emit insert mutation if missing
Implement Rule 4: blank line separation — scan block elements, ensure blank line between adjacent paragraphs and block elements
Implement Rule 5: trailing whitespace — scan each line via the line-starts index, emit delete mutation for trailing spaces
Write unit tests for each rule in em-core/src/formatter.rs covering at least the happy path and one edge case per rule
Run cargo test -p em-core and confirm all formatter tests pass

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo test -p em-core --test formatter passes with explicit test cases for list continuation, table alignment, heading spacing, blank-line separation, and trailing whitespace trim (5+ tests)
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test -p em-core --test formatter 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. ([5-9]|[1-9][0-9])'

acceptanceCriteria.text: format() is idempotent — running it twice on the same input produces the same output
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test -p em-core --test formatter format_is_idempotent 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. [1-9]'

acceptanceCriteria.text: format() does not change the semantic content — only whitespace, list markers, and alignment are adjusted
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test -p em-core --test formatter format_preserves_semantics 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok\. [1-9]'

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo test --workspace exits 0 with no failures and no baseline regression
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -qE 'test result: ok' && ! cargo test --workspace 2>&1 | grep -qE 'FAILED'

items.title: Phase0-commonmark-ci: CommonMark spec suite wired into CI with skip-list
items.intent: Every CI run reports exactly how many CommonMark spec tests pass, how many are in the documented skip-list, and zero unexplained failures. Any test that fails without a skip-list entry breaks the build. Known tree-sitter divergences are named, documented, and tracked for upstream contribution.
items.description: Wire the official CommonMark spec test suite (spec.json from the commonmark-spec repo) into the em-core test harness. The test runner parses each spec test's markdown input through em-core's parse() function, renders the output, and compares against the expected HTML. Maintain an explicit skip-list at em-core/tests/commonmark_skip.json — each entry must include the spec test number, the failing input fragment, and a one-sentence reason (e.g. 'tree-sitter lazy continuation divergence, see issue #N'). CI fails if any test fails and is NOT in the skip-list. CI also fails if a skip-listed test starts passing (a signal to remove it from the list). Document the current pass rate in docs/commonmark_status.md.
steps: Download or vendor spec.json from the commonmark-spec repository into em-core/tests/fixtures/
Write em-core/tests/commonmark.rs that reads spec.json and runs each test through parse()
Implement a minimal HTML renderer in the test harness (or use a reference renderer) to produce output for comparison
Add em-core/tests/commonmark_skip.json — start with an empty array, add entries as failures are discovered
Update the test runner to fail on any unskipped failure and warn on any skip-listed test that now passes
Add the commonmark test job to the GitHub Actions workflow
Run the suite, identify all failures, populate the skip-list with documented reasons
Create docs/commonmark_status.md with the current pass/fail/skipped counts

acceptanceCriteria.text: em-core/tests/fixtures/spec.json exists (CommonMark 0.31.2 spec examples)
acceptanceCriteria.verify: test -f em-core/tests/fixtures/spec.json && jq -e '. | length > 600' em-core/tests/fixtures/spec.json

acceptanceCriteria.text: em-core/tests/commonmark_skip.json exists listing known divergences, each with a reason
acceptanceCriteria.verify: test -f em-core/tests/commonmark_skip.json && jq -e 'all(.[]; .reason != null)' em-core/tests/commonmark_skip.json

acceptanceCriteria.text: cargo test -p em-core --test commonmark runs the spec suite and reports pass/fail/skip counts
acceptanceCriteria.verify: cargo test -p em-core --test commonmark 2>&1 | grep -qiE 'commonmark.*(pass|fail|skip).*[0-9]+'

acceptanceCriteria.text: docs/commonmark_status.md exists and documents the current pass rate as a committed number
acceptanceCriteria.verify: test -f docs/commonmark_status.md && grep -qE '[0-9]+%|[0-9]+/[0-9]+' docs/commonmark_status.md

acceptanceCriteria.text: A GitHub Actions workflow job runs the commonmark test and fails if the pass rate drops below the committed number
acceptanceCriteria.verify: grep -rqE 'commonmark' .github/workflows/ && yq '.jobs | to_entries | .[] | select(.value | tostring | test("commonmark"))' .github/workflows/*.yml 2>/dev/null | grep -q .
